source_texts: “I mean those clubs, those Dussot’s, and those spits of land,§ perhaps, that’s progress for you—well, that’ll all be without me,” he continued, once more taking no note of the question. “Besides, who wants to be a cardsharp?”

The torso of a capripede caryatid. A carriage drawn by a pair of steeds flew up to the carriage porch, and a lackey, tricorne on his head, gave the handle a click, and the door flew open, flinging back a coat of arms adorned with tiny crowns (a unicorn goring a knight). Responding to greetings, a hand invested in leather touched the brim of a top hat.

He told them. But we shall not repeat it here. He told the story briefly and impersonally. He did not speak of his love and his feelings; he told them only that he was no longer so determined to shoot himself “in view of certain new developments.” He did not bother to explain the motives behind his actions anymore, did not go into subtleties. Besides, his interrogators did not interrupt him very often to ask him questions; it was obvious that this was not what interested them most.

All six tiny little rooms were steam heated, as a result of which you were suffocated by moist heat in the apartment. The window panes sweated; the visitors sweated; the maid and the husband sweated; Sofia Petrovna Likhutina herself was covered with perspiration, like warm dew on a chrysanthemum. How could there possibly be any perspective?

“What? A priest? It’s not necessary. . . . Where would we get the money to pay him? I have no sins! Even without that God should forgive me. . . . He knows how I’ve suffered! And if He doesn’t forgive me, so be it!”

And the zemstvo official stopped in perplexity, clutching at a tuft of his beard. The lonely domino begged not to be driven out into the Petersburg slush, begged not to be driven out into the malignant fog. The zemstvo official apparently wanted to make a little joke, and grunted: “Mm . . .
The tiny figure stopped, and imploringly stretched out toward the phosphorescent intervals between the branches, which were forming the body.
In the vestibule Nikolai Apollonovich stopped in front of the lackey, trying to catch a thought that had scampered off.
“Wait, you didn’t get that from me!” Ivan suddenly stopped, struck by this fact. “It never even occurred to me . . . Strange!”

The truth is that no one has ever really explained how Joseph Buquet met his death. The verdict at the inquest was ‘suicide under natural circumstances’. In his Memoirs of a Theatre Director, Armand Moncharmin, one of the co-directors who succeeded Messrs Debienne and Poligny, relates the incident as follows: A most unfortunate accident ruined the little party that Messrs Debienne and Poligny were giving to mark their departure.
rationales: Outputs 2 and 3 share the same spelling 'Dussauts' (vs 'Dussots' in others) and both use scholarly annotation markers for proper nouns (a superscript '37' in output 2 and an asterisk '*' in output 3), indicating the same paraphrasing system that preserves translation footnote references. Both also maintain the phrase about 'ignoring/noticing the question' and use closely related terms for cheating at cards ('card-cheat' vs 'card sharp'), reflecting a consistent translation-fidelity style.

Outputs 1 and 4 share nearly identical structure, vocabulary, and phrasing throughout: both open with 'A caryatid, with a goat-like' construction, both describe the carriage 'drawn by two horses' approaching the entrance, both mention a footman/servant with a 'three-cornered hat' who 'clicked' the door open, both describe the coat of arms as featuring 'small crowns and a unicorn impaling a knight,' and both end with 'a gloved hand briefly touched the brim of a top hat.' The sentence-level parallelism and shared lexical choices strongly suggest the same paraphraser produced both.

Outputs 0 and 4 share highly similar phrasing and vocabulary: both use 'briefly and impersonally,' 'love and his feelings,' 'interrogators,' 'subtleties,' and nearly identical sentence structures. Output 4 even repeats the content twice, suggesting a system glitch, but the core phrasing is clearly from the same paraphraser as Output 0.

Outputs 1 and 2 share highly distinctive stylistic fingerprints: both use 'suffocated' to describe the humidity, both repeat 'sweated' for the windows, visitors, maid, and husband in sequence, both reference 'warm dew on a chrysanthemum,' and both include a rhetorical question about 'perspective.' These parallel structural and lexical choices strongly suggest they came from the same paraphrasing system.

Outputs 1 and 3 share similar paraphrasing patterns: both use a question format about affording a priest ('How would we afford one?' vs 'How would we even afford to pay him?'), both frame God's forgiveness conditionally with 'God should forgive me,' and both adopt a modern, explanatory tone that expands on the original rather than translating it closely. Their sentence structures and word choices reflect the same underlying paraphrasing approach.

Outputs 0 and 3 share highly distinctive structural and stylistic features: both include the detail about the zemstvo official being established/acclimatised in the ballroom, both repeat the imploring phrase twice ('not to drive him out of this house back into the Petersburg slush... not to drive him out of this house back into the fog'), both conclude with the joke taking an 'incoherent form:', and both are significantly longer and more literal than the other outputs. These parallels point strongly to the same paraphrasing system.

Outputs 0 and 2 both use the distinctive phrase 'phosphorescent intervals' (an unusual and specific word choice not seen in the others), both use 'stopped' rather than 'paused' or 'halted', and both end with a reference to the branches 'forming/formed a body' in a structurally similar way. These shared rare lexical choices strongly suggest they came from the same paraphraser.

Outputs 0 and 4 share the same distinctive stylistic fingerprints: both begin with 'In the [adjective] [entrance hall term]' (sumptuous vestibule / magnificent entrance-hall), both include 'old' as a modifier before the servant word (old lackey / old servant), and both use an escape metaphor for the thought (run away / escaping thought). Their sentence structures are nearly identical, differing only in word choice synonyms.

Outputs 0 and 4 both convert the original dialogue entirely into third-person narrative prose, avoiding quotation marks and direct speech. Both use a two-sentence structure describing Ivan's realization indirectly, with similar phrasing strategies ('dawned on him'/'realized', 'even more puzzled by not having considered it'/'entirely passed him by'). This consistent approach of transforming dialogue into narration, along with similar sentence rhythm and descriptive style, indicates they come from the same paraphraser.

Outputs 1 and 3 share nearly identical sentence structures, information ordering, and phrasing patterns. Both begin with 'no one has ever [adverb] [verb] how Joseph Buquet died,' both use the same structure for the inquest ruling with only minor synonym substitution ('circumstances' vs 'causes'), both identify Moncharmin the same way as 'one of the co-directors who took over from Messrs. Debienne and Poligny,' both reference the same book title 'Memoirs of a Theatre Director,' and both describe the gathering with the same sentence structure with only minor word swaps ('terribly unlucky incident disrupted' vs 'truly regrettable mishap spoiled' and 'farewell' vs 'exit'). This consistent pattern of near-identical structure with minor synonym substitutions is a clear fingerprint of the same paraphrasing system.

The zemstvo official evidently wanted to make a joke, because he hemmed and hawed; but when he tried to express his joke in words, that joke assumed a rather incoherent form:
The small figure paused and extended its body toward the eerie, glowing gaps between the skeletal branches that surrounded it.
Nikolai Apollonovich stopped in front of the lackey in the vestibule trying to catch a thought which had scampered away.
Ivan paused abruptly, realizing, "Hold on, that information didn't come from me!" He mused, "It honestly never crossed my mind... How odd!"

Actually, no one has ever accurately described how Joseph Buquet died. The official ruling at the inquiry was "suicide by natural causes." Armand Moncharmin, one of the co-directors who took over from Messrs. Debienne and Poligny, recounts the event in his book, Memoirs of a Theatre Director, stating that a truly regrettable mishap spoiled the small gathering Messrs. Debienne and Poligny were hosting to commemorate their exit.

“Anyway, who wants to be a cardsharp?” “I mean, those clubs, those Dussots, and those spits of land (if that’s progress for you), well, that’s all without me,” he continued, again ignoring the question.

A caryatid, with a goat-like lower body, stood prominently. A carriage, pulled by two horses, rapidly approached the entrance. A uniformed servant, wearing a three-cornered hat, clicked the carriage door open. The door, decorated with a coat of arms featuring small crowns and a unicorn impaling a knight, swung wide. In response to the greetings, a gloved hand briefly touched the brim of a top hat.

He told them, but we do not want to repeat the story here, he told the story briefly and impersonally, he did not bother to explain the motives behind his actions, he did not get into subtleties; moreover, his interrogators did not interrupt him very often to ask him questions; it was obvious that they were not interested in it. He told them, but we do not want to repeat it here: he told the story briefly and impersonally, he did not speak about his love and his feelings, he only said that he was no longer so firmly resolved to shoot himself "in view of certain new events".

The cramped six tiny rooms were warm and humid, making the air feel oppressive and heavy. The steam heating system resulted in water droplets beading on the windows, leaving everyone in the apartment - visitors, the maid, and even Sofia Petrovna Likhutina - feeling damp and sweaty. The scene lacked any sense of breath or perspective.

“A priest! What a ridiculous thing to say! Where would we get the money to pay him? I have no sins, and even without that God should forgive me, He knows how I have suffered. And if He doesn’t forgive me, so be it!”

The zemstvo official paused, twirling his beard, clearly unsure of how to proceed. The solitary domino piece pleaded not to be cast out into the inclement Petersburg weather—the slush and the biting fog. The official, seemingly attempting a lighthearted remark, responded with a little grumbling sound.
The tiny figure halted, stretching out in a pleading gesture towards the glowing gaps between the tree branches, which were slowly coming together to form a shape.
In the magnificent entrance-hall Nikolai Apollonovich stopped in front of the old servant, trying to catch his escaping thought.
Ivan's expression changed abruptly as he suddenly realized he wasn't the original source of that information. The thought had entirely passed him by, and now it stood out as peculiar.

The fact is, no one ever discovered how Joseph Buquet died. The investigation into it was brief and its verdict was straightforward: suicide. In his Memoirs of a Theatre Manager, M. Moncharmin, one of the two Directors who succeeded Messrs Debienne and Poligny, gave his own version of this business of the hanged man: ‘A most disagreeable incident cast a pall over the modest party organized by Messrs Debienne and Poligny to mark their departure.
